We can make the separation of "json" and "ndjson" (either through a boolean argument or through two methods) be a separate PR, after this one. When that's done, the `std::pair<int, const BuilderPtr>` return type can become just `BuilderPtr`, as discussed in a previous comment (above, not part of this review).

The only thing I'd ask for in this PR is to avoid duplicating the NumpyBuffersContainer class. It can go in a header file (with implementation). It's more than a code-cleanliness/DRY thing (although that is important): when working on the specialized JSON parser last week, I found that Windows _randomly_ segfaulted when two classes in different parts of the codebase had the same name. It was a hard-to-diagnose crash because it was failing in a test for the other class with this name—the mere existence of the new class crashed it (because it was existing with a bad name!).

Missing resolution for NGSPICE_MANUAL_URL, which suffers from same problem.

Additionally, this would be a good place to add one more fix for when user specifies ngspice version <=31. In those versions, the DLL naming did not contain a version number. In lines 230-240 (223-234 before pull request), if downloaded DLL's name is already 'ngspice.dll', the unlink command causes the DLL to be deleted, and results in error. Replacing those lines with the code below adds a check to prevent deletion if the dll-vs subdirectory only contains 'ngspice.dll'.

```
if dll_path.joinpath(src).exists():
    if target.exists():
        target.unlink()
    try:
        target.symlink_to(src)
    except OSError:
        # OSError: symbolic link privilege not held
        # Windows: If User Account Control (UAC) is on, any user with the "Create Symbolic
        #   Links" privilege that is not in the Administrators group can simply create a
        #   symbolic link.  For users within the Administrators group and with UAC on, the user
        #   must "Run as Administrator".
        shutil.copy(target.parent.joinpath(src), target)
```

Did a usage first pass, I have a few high-level pubsub design questions before I dig deeper.

It looks like we're using a channel abstraction in order to be able to piggyback off of shared publisher <--> subscriber long-polling connections, where the publisher and subscriber interfaces expose the channel concept to the application code via the channel type and deal only with a generic `PubMessage` message type that oneofs across the messages for the different channels. This then entails pass-through methods to the subinterfaces for each channel, maintaining channel --> data structure maps internally, and leaking what is essentially a transport detail (the long-polling connection) to the application level by requiring publishers/subscribers to specify the channel type. I'm wondering if there's a better way to structure this such that the application code doesn't have to worry about channels and more of this logic is encoded in the type system so we have compile-time guarantees that things are 👌 .

What if we adopted a pattern similar to gRPC's shared transport channel, generalized from managing a 1-to-1 connection to managing 1-to-n connections, where we have a transport broker concept that encapsulates the publisher <--> subscriber long-polling connections. The subscriber interface for each pubsub instance takes a shared pointer to the broker on construction, and can therefore share across different pubsub instances (e.g. separate subscriber objects for `WAIT_FOR_OBJECT_EVICTION` and `WAIT_FOR_REF_REMOVED`), where new publisher <--> subscriber connections can be registered by any of the pubsub instances.

subscribers <--> broker <--transport--> broker <--> publishers

I think there could be a few advantages here:

1. We could then expose a subscriber interface per pubsub instance, templated on the concrete message type (no need for a proto oneof message to be exposed to the application code, that could be isolated to the broker), which will give better compile-time checking of the application-level pubsub messages and will obviate the need for those passthrough methods in a top-level subscriber interface that requires a channel type to be specified.
2. I think that you'd still need a proto-level channel type enum, but I think that you could get away with specializing the subscriber with _the enum value itself_. E.g. `Subscriber<typename MessageID, typename Message, rpc::ChannelType channel_type>` could be specialized as `Subscriber<ObjectID, rpc::WaitForObjectEvictionMessage, rpc::WAIT_FOR_OBJECT_EVICTION>`, where the `channel_type` is included when interacting with the broker (e.g. subscribe, unsubscribe). Dispatch of messages received in the long-poll response by the broker will still have to use a channel --> subscriber table, but that can be populated dynamically when the subscriber is constructed: `broker_.RegisterSubscriber(channel_type, this)`.
3. The transport-level optimization of sharing long-polling connections is pushed down as an implementation detail of the communication broker concept, and is a natural place to evolve other transport-level details, like switching to a bidi streaming connection or switching to a centralized message broker.
4. Other transport-level things, like the core worker client pool and the subscriber address/port, can be moved to the broker. Publisher addresses will probably continue to be required by subscriber methods, unless we want the broker to always map worker or node IDs to concrete addresses (probably less efficient in some cases?).
5. The current process of registering a new channel will be replaced by adding a new channel type to the proto enum, templating a subscriber with a new message type and the channel type, and reusing the existing broker.

I think that you would have an analogous broker abstraction on the publisher side as well (encapsulating the cached long-polling connection, controlling the message batching), although I haven't thought it through as much since I think it should be simpler than the subscriber-side.

The cons with this broker approach, as I see them:

1. Broker concept is exposed to the application level, although what's needed to configure that concept (worker client pool, subscriber address/port, etc.) is already exposed to the application level, so I'm not sure if this is any worse?
2. A shared transport-level broker will obviously have to be thread-safe, although that shouldn't be very difficult.
3. Hidden dragons that I haven't thought of.

Lmk what you think about this approach and whether I'm missing anything! This is also something that could wait until a future PR, e.g. when I port the OBOD object location subscriptions or the GCS node/actor tables to this pubsub I'm sure that I'll have to refactor some stuff anyway.

This is intended, since the `--` separator is used by bash to signify the end of the command's options, in this case it marks the end of the `ray submit` options, so all remaining arguments will instead be passed to the underlying script when executed, as the commend above this line indicates.

Otherwise, the shell could interpret the `--arg1` argument as being an option for the `ray submit` command, which would fail with an unknown argument error, or worse, silently execute with the wrong behavior if `--arg1` _is_ a valid argument for `ray submit`.

This is a common pattern used in other CLIs, e.g. Bazel, where when running a target script/binary `foo` that also requires arguments, it would be provided via `bazel run //:foo -- arg1 arg2`.

Hi @aryamanJgl, thanks for this nice PR!

A few comments/questions from my side:
- I would typically create such finite set constraints as:
```py
z = cp.Variable(len(finite_set), boolean=True)
constraints = [
    cp.sum(cp.multiply(finite_set,z)) == x,
    cp.sum(z) == 1
]
```
This replaces the `n-2` inequality + `1` equality constraints with `2` equality constraints, where `n` is the size of the set. Additionally, (although not currently implemented by CVXPY), some solvers add special support for such [SOS1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Special_ordered_set) constraints. Did you consider such an implementation as well?
- You can vectorized the inequality constraints as `z[1:] <= z[:-1]`
- Perhaps the changes to the solving chain should go in `_reductions_for_problem_class()`

Thanks for the contribution.
The PyMeasure instruments section provides a number of features that make programming and maintaining an instrument easier. I've remarked upon a few in the file, but do also reccomend you to take a look at this part of the documentation: https://pymeasure.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dev/adding_instruments.html.

A lot of properties can converted into a `control`, `measurement`, or `setting` property. For those that cannot be converted into this, I would recommend using `self.write` in stead of `self.switch_data.write` and `self.values` (or in some cases `self.ask`) in stead of `self.switch_data.query` (though, if at all possible, it is always recomended to use the aforementioned properties).

Apart from that, you could also have a look at how the Keithley 2700 or 2750 is implemented, these are also matrix multiplexers and might be helpful to look at.

Finally, don't forget to add the instrument to __init__.py file in the Keysight folder, add the required documentation files in the docs/api/instruments/keysight folder, and add yourself to the author lists (one at AUTHORS.txt at the root of the repo and one in docs/about/authors.rst).

If you have any questions or anything is not (completely) clear, feel free to let me know!

I only have medium familiarity with MLflow, so I don't have any strong opinions about what the integration should look like, but wanted to raise a set of questions here for discussion.

If I understand correctly, typically an entire MLflow run lives inside an single process, and this diff enables an MLflow run to span multiple processes.  How "blessed" of a workflow is that?  If I'm understanding correctly, in some situations this diff uses `start_run` to connect to a run that has already started. Is MLflow likely to continue supporting that usage?

For someone who has written code that uses MLflow, would they be able to wrap it inside Dagster?  Or would they need to change all their `mlflow.log_param` invocations to `context.resources.mlflow.log_param`?  Would it be possible to make it work out of the box?  E.g. with something like:
```
@resource(...)
def mlflow_tracking(...):
    with mlflow.start_run():
        yield
```

What about users who want to run multiple MLflow runs inside a single Dagster run?  E.g. perhaps they want to do launch a hyperparameter grid search with Dagster dynamic orchestration where each mapped solid trains a model.

Before we stop running those on PRs, do we have a safe and reliable mechanism to be alerted when those start failing on `main` as well?

The problem here would actually relate to session lifecycles rather than session usage. We've documented this [here](https://github.com/talkiq/gcloud-aio/tree/master/storage#usage), specifically:
```
async with Storage() as client:
    # closes the client.session on leaving the context manager

# OR

client = Storage()
# do stuff
await client.close()  # close the session explicitly
```

That said, it took me a while to dig this example up, seems we only included it in the README for _one_ of the packages here rather than all of them. I'll go update those -- all of the `gcloud-aio-*` libraries should be equivalent in this regard!

```python
from photutils import CircularAperture

data = np.ones((50, 50))
ap1 = CircularAperture(((10, 20), (30, 40)), r=3)
ap2 = CircularAperture(((10, 20), (30, 40)), r=5)
ap3 = CircularAperture((30, 20), r=5)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 5))
ax.imshow(data, cmap='Greys')
ap1_patches = ap1.plot(ax, color='red', label='Aper 1')
ap2_patches = ap2.plot(ax, color='blue', label='Aper 2')
ap3_patches = ap3.plot(ax, color='green', label='Aper 3')
handles = (ap1_patches[0], ap2_patches[0], ap3_patches[0])
ax.legend(handles=handles)
```

I don't support adding checks like this to `Booster.predict()`. This extra overhead might have a noticeable impact on time-per-prediction if you're serving a LightGBM model and doing single-row predictions.

I understand that "columns are in the wrong order" is a failure mode to guard against in deploying machine learning models, but I'm not convinced that the `Booster` object (or even the `lightgbm` library) is the right place for such checks. Doing this in the way currently proposed in this PR makes the overhead of this check unavoidable, which latency-sensitive users might not be happy about.

I feel the same way about requests like #4040.

If @StrikerRUS @shiyu1994 disagree and are fine with adding things like this, I won't block this PR.

I'll also raise an alternative idea here...what about something like the following?

* `Booster.predict()` = optimized for performance
* `Booster.predict_strict()` = same as `Booster.predict()`, but optimized for more input data checks. Raises informative errors and warnings when the data being scored is different from the training data.

These filters should be unit tested (there are tests for the test name filter).

Additionally, I don't think we should introduce more filter fields, but rather should extend our existing filter field to more options. I've raised this offline, but my proposal would be something like this:

```
filters = filter_str.split(" ")
for filter in filters:
    if filter.startswith("name:"):
        value = filter[5:]
        apply_filters = NameFilter(value)
    elif filter.startswith("team:"):
        value = filter[5:]
        apply_filters = TeamFilter(value)
    # ...
    else:
        # Default (no prefix): name regex
        apply_filters = NameRegexFilter(filter)
```

For each test we can then loop through the apply_filters and only proceed if all match. Regular filters would be `in` filters, i.e. `name:xgboost` should match `xgboost_train_small` and also `tune_xgboost_sweep`. Name regex filter should work like right now as a regex match (not search). This is useful to keep backwards compatibility but also to allow e.g. specifying several tests, e.g. via `long_running_many_drivers|tune_network_throughput` which currently works.

Let's discuss this before proceeding with this PR.

I've played with the example and looked through most of the code and I'm super impressed! Great job Lisa! I wouldn't change very much, just the default behaviour for old users and introduce a very conservative suggestion for the ``global_constraints``.

## Naming schemes

I agree with your naming: we need ``investment_periods`` to distinguish from ``snapshot_periods`` that run through the year. Old PyPSA "overnight" behaviour is often called "multi-period" optimisation, but we need to make clear that we also do multiple investment periods here.

## Default behaviour

The guiding principle is that old users shouldn't have to make any big changes, and that there should be no surprises.

I suggest that n.snapshots stays the same, and can be either MultiIndex or Index.

All PyPSA functions should by default treat MultiIndex snapshots just the same as Index snapshots. This is the case for old PyPSA ``n.lopf(pyomo=False)``, but ``n.lopf(pyomo=True)``, ``n.lpf()`` and ``n.pf()`` all break when using MultiIndex - we should fix this in future releases (but not now).

The multi-period optimisation is the only one to treat MultiIndex differently, and must be activated with:

``n.lopf(pyomo=False, multi_investment_periods=True)``

When this is activated, it checks that ``n.snapshots`` is MultiIndex, that level 0 is integer, and that the integers are increasing. It also checks that all investment periods appear in the index of ``n.investment_period_weightings`` and vice-versa, throwing an error if not.

If ``n.lopf(pyomo=True, multi_investment_periods=True)`` is called, an error is thrown.

### Snapshot weightings

This is a parallel change to the investment periods. In PyPSA Version 0.18.0 we will replace the pd.Series ``n.snapshot_weightings`` with a pd.DataFrame with three columns ``[objective_weightings, generator_weightings, store_weightings]``. This separates out the three current use cases of weightings in the LOPF:

- ``objective_weightings``: for operational costs in the objective function
- ``store_weightings``: for elapsed time in the filling of ``Store`` and ``StorageUnit`` components
- ``generator_weightings``: for the contribution of generators to ``GlobalConstraint`` components for e.g. CO2 budgets

Example use cases:

- 3-hourly sampling for full year: ``objective_weighting = generator_weighting = store_weighting = 3``.
- hourly sampling for 10 days to represent full year with cyclic storage: ``objective weighting = generator_weighting = 52. , 
store_weighting = 1.``

BUT this case fails if e.g. a ``Store`` represents non-cyclic depletion of e.g. biomass or some other finite resource, in which case you want ``store_weighting = 52``, like ``generator_weighting``. This interface needs all storage doing short-term cycling, or all storage non-cyclic (depleting a finite resource). We should fix this when we implement Kotzur's seasonal storage scheme.

### Investment period weightings

I think the way Lisa implemented this looks good, maybe change the names of weightings to be similar to the ``snapshot_weightings``?

I also suggest not to have ``n.investment_periods``, but track these only via level 0 of ``n.snapshots``.

### Behaviour when an empty network is generated

If you call ``n = pypsa.Network()`` by default will be created:

- ``n.snapshots = pd.Index(["now"])``
- ``n.snapshot_weightings = pd.DataFrame([[1.,1.,1.]],index=["now"],
                                     columns=["objective_weightings","store_weightings","generator_weightings"])``
- ``n.investment_period_weightings = pd.DataFrame(columns=["objective_weightings","store_weightings","generator_weightings"])``

### Behaviour when a network from PyPSA Version < 0.18.0 is imported

Create new empty ``n.investment_period_weightings`` like for empty network above.

The values of pd.Series ``n.snapshot_weightings`` are copied across all three values of the new pd.DataFrame ``n.snapshot_weightings``.

## Global constraints for ``p_nom_max`` at each bus, carrier and investment_period

I suggest to keep these all in ``n.global_constraints`` since they are global in the sense of affecting multiple components at once. We need to add to the ``GlobalConstraint`` table attributes ``bus``, ``carrier`` and ``investment_period`` and type ``multi_investment_period_p_nom_max`` or similar.

Yes, this will create lots of global constraints, but introducing e.g. ``n.global_constraints_p`` doesn't help since you need 3-d information.

This way is minimally invasive.

## Treatment of sub_networks

I think the current way is good. The sub_networks are generated as needed, and the last one to be generated is stored. There is no need to store them from all investment periods, i.e. no need for ``n.sub_networks_p``.

The sub_network is anyway more of a hidden variable.

## Storage behaviour between investment periods

Did I understand correctly that cyclic storage is cyclic within each investment period? This behaviour would be fine.

If the storage is not cyclic, is energy transfered between investment periods? Probably this is not such a good idea for e.g. depletable biomass. But maybe we should model depletable biomass as a generator, rather than as a store that starts full. Then the generator can fill the storage at the start of each year?

## Check objective function

Since the objective function is quite complicated now, please check for some non-trivial examples that the Gurobi objective function is also what you calculated from the results. This should help to find bugs.

## Coding style

Try to avoid abbreviations like ``inv_p`` and ``sns`` in API functions like ``network.determine_network_topology``. Here we should be explicit for understandability. You can abbreviate inside functions, that's fine.

Also try to avoid committing code very specific to your own setup, like paths to libraries.

I like the concept. Have you tried it on pub/sub yet? I'm not sure how well the change to have `send_command` return a response future will work with the pub/sub code, which
(a) doesn't immediately retrieve responses to subscribe commands, instead leaving them for the message handler to pick up
(b) needs to be able to handle unsolicited messages.

Possibly the Protocol could just put responses into an asyncio.Queue, and `read_response` could then pull a response from the queue. If the queue gets too full (which might just be 2 responses), use `pause_reading` to stop more data being added until it shrinks again. But I don't know if that will preserve the ordering one needs to make pipelining work, and it might also reduce performance again.

Do you have any thoughts on implementing the `pause_writing` and `resume_writing` callbacks? I can't think of anything useful they could do, since there is no way to apply back-pressure to a client that is sending lots of commands without awaiting responses.

I'm assuming the commented-out code is things you need to revisit before finalising things.

OK, here we go! I'll start by repeating what I said above. This is very high quality contribution! Thanks for taking the time to research and understand the issue thoroughly, and then posting a high quality solution! I've learnt a lot reviewing and checking the background context needed. Thanks again!

You'll see plenty of comments, but in general the main points are:
* The `context` object might not need the `is_prelease` method there, since a check for the PackageRecord, MatchSpec or VersionOrder objects might be enough.
* I am concerned about the ramifications of the `limit` mode, which is [the default mode suggested by PEP440](https://peps.python.org/pep-0440/#handling-of-pre-releases). If we implement this, we need to decide how we are going to interpret the PEP440 directives, specially when it comes to the "allow pre-releases for packages already using a pre-release" directive, if we understand it in a package-by-package basis. This of course would need specific tests.
* How we handle `*`-containing versions.

I am also curious about what happens to projects that are _only_ available as pre-releases (for now). The most popular example is `black`. I think `pip` handles this with an exception, but it still begs the question: should a prerelease-only project be always available regardless the `prerelease=xxxx` option chosen? PEP440 seems to be addressing this in the 'limit'  mode implementation but, again, we need tests for that too.

On top of this, I checked open issues in `pypa/pip` and other tools, to see what shortcomings the community might have found before us. Please take a look at the following references and let's see if we can establish whether the current status of the PR suffers from the same issue(s):

* Intersection with other flags and/or commands? https://github.com/pypa/pip/issues/8983
* This part of the PEP440 'limit' mode: https://github.com/pypa/pip/issues/9121
* This other issue about automated opt-in via the specifier. It sounds like pip enables RCs if the specifier mentions RCs, but it shoudl only mean so for the current stable branch, not every subsequent RC? https://github.com/pypa/pip/issues/7579

Last but not least, a ramification of this feature. Allowing prereleases has effects on two levels: creating the index and solving the environment. On the index creation side, it determines the availability of the package. The solving side means whether we pick it or not. In that vein, wonder if we should extend the availability checks to `conda search`. 

Apologies for the length of the review! This is an extremely useful and important feature, and I am very grateful we are progressing in the right direction. Thanks again for the nth time :)

I won't block, but the strategy employed here doesn't seem scalable.

> Agree that this is weird, but I think that this weirdness is just inherited from the weirdness of @logger. In a world where there were only handlers, the two responsibilities would be very distinct: DagsterLogManager is a log that produces messages with Dagster metadata and has a set of handlers on it. DagsterLogConversionHandler is a special handler that sneakily converts normal python logs into Dagster-formatted logs, and then calls all of DagsterLogManager's handlers with this new record.

Just spitballin' here: what if we relied on the Python logging framework for dispatching instead of implementing on our own in `DagsterLogManager`.  E.g.  `context.log` would point to an object produced by invoking `logging.getLogger("__dagster__")`.  Then, for each Dagster `LoggerDefinition`, we'd create a logging `Handler` that wraps the `Logger` returned by the `LoggerDefinition`, and have that `Handler` subscribe to the `"__dagster__"` logger?
